dataset.name: IBM AML-Data HI-Small synthetic alert benchmark

primary_candidate.name: logistic_regression_raw_signals
primary_candidate.notes: Primary sanity-check candidate: raw alert signals with chronology-safe history, excluding label-derived rate encodings.

ablations.name: ablation_amount_related
ablations.note: Removed 10 columns: total_amount, max_amount, mean_amount, min_amount, median_amount, std_amount, amount_range, amount_per_transaction ...

ablations.name: ablation_normalized_amount
ablations.note: Removed 12 columns: total_amount_usd, max_amount_usd, mean_amount_usd, min_amount_usd, median_amount_usd, std_amount_usd, amount_range_usd, amount_per_transaction_usd ...

ablations.name: ablation_history_chronology
ablations.note: Removed 11 columns: source_account_hours_since_prev_alert, source_account_prior_alert_count, source_account_prior_total_amount_usd, source_account_prior_avg_amount_usd, source_account_seen_train, source_account_frequency_train, source_bank_hours_since_prev_alert, source_bank_prior_alert_count ...

ablations.name: ablation_counterparty_breadth
ablations.note: Removed 9 columns: unique_destination_accounts, unique_destination_banks, log_unique_destination_accounts, counterparty_density, repeated_counterparty_ratio, top_counterparty_tx_share, top_counterparty_amount_share, same_bank_ratio ...

ablations.name: ablation_time_cadence
ablations.note: Removed 12 columns: time_span_hours, avg_gap_hours, max_gap_hours, tx_per_hour, created_hour, created_day_of_week, hour_sin, hour_cos ...

ablations.name: ablation_payment_currency_mix
ablations.note: Removed 10 columns: currency_count, has_mixed_currencies, payment_format_count, ach_ratio, wire_ratio, cash_ratio, cheque_ratio, credit_card_ratio ...

findings.finding: Train-only positive-rate encodings are directly derived from labels and are not acceptable as the primary externally-cited benchmark feature set.

Train-only pattern-rate encodings are derived from pattern annotations that originate from the laundering-attempt file and should be treated as benchmark convenience rather than bank-realistic signal.

Chronology-safe history features are generated via `cumcount`, shifted cumulative totals, and previous timestamps on data sorted by alert `created_at`; this uses only prior rows in the ordered stream, not future alerts.

Alert labels are defined as `1 if any transaction in the grouped window is laundering`. The same grouped window also determines amount, breadth, and cadence features, so the task is structurally easier than real alert adjudication and likely benefits from synthetic grouping convenience.

Many alerts share identical timestamps. Prior-alert history among same-timestamp rows therefore depends on deterministic row ordering rather than true event latency. This is not future leakage, but it is a synthetic artifact.

split_robustness.name: chronological_50_25_25
split_robustness.note: Larger later holdout; stresses generalization to more future data.

split_robustness.name: chronological_60_10_30_later_only
split_robustness.note: Later-only stress split with final 30% held out for testing.

li_generalization.reason: LI-Small files are not present in the local IBM dataset directory; cross-illicit-ratio transfer could not be run in this repo-only pass.

randomization.note: If this stays high, the pipeline is leaking. If it collapses near base-rate behavior, supervision path is behaving sensibly.

baseline_results.name: chronological_queue
baseline_results.notes: Chronological queue ordered by created_at ascending.

baseline_results.name: weighted_signal_heuristic
baseline_results.notes: Validation-tuned weighted heuristic over normalized amount, transaction count, and counterparty breadth.

summary_points: The primary raw-signal champion remains far above the amount baseline under the default chronological split.
Shuffled-label control collapses performance and argues against an implementation bug that directly leaks labels through the supervision path.
There is still benchmark convenience: the proxy label is defined on the same grouped transaction window that also determines the strongest ranking features.
Pattern-derived exact-match information remains a leakage-like convenience signal and should stay flagged even when its marginal impact is small.
The primary score is strong enough for internal synthetic benchmarking discussions, but not strong enough to cite externally as evidence of real bank AML ranking performance.
